response: Enforced in May 2018, the European Union’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) applies to any company processing EU residents’ personal data regardless of the company’s location, with core rules including the “right to be forgotten” (data erasure), data portability, explicit consent requirements for processing, mandatory Data Protection Officers for large-scale handling, 72-hour breach reporting mandates, and maximum fines of 4% of global annual revenue or 20 million euros (whichever is higher). The GDPR has spurred major enforcement actions—such as a 2023 1.2 billion euro fine against Meta for transferring EU user data to the U.S., a 746 million euro fine against Amazon for ad targeting practices, and over 200 million euros in total fines against Google—and has inspired analogous global privacy laws, including California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act. Critics argue the GDPR imposes disproportionate compliance burdens on small businesses, stifles innovation, has led to user-frustrating cookie consent banners, and suffers inconsistent enforcement across EU member states, but the regulation has nonetheless established data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global standard for data protection legislation.
